stem: A network engineer monitors packet loss rates on a data center switch. Over 24 hours, they collect packet loss measurements every minute (1,440 observations). The observed mean packet loss rate is 0.3% with an observed standard deviation of 0.15%. However, the engineer suspects the data exhibits three issues: (1) temporal autocorrelation — consecutive measurements are correlated with lag-1 autocorrelation between 0.4 and 0.7, (2) a diurnal pattern — loss rates are systematically 0.1% higher during peak hours (8am-8pm, about 720 observations) than off-peak, and (3) 23 measurements show packet loss above 1.5%, likely due to micro-bursts rather than baseline conditions. Estimate the true baseline (off-peak, non-burst) packet loss rate and provide a confidence interval that correctly accounts for autocorrelation, the diurnal pattern, and the contaminating outliers.

ground_truth.answer: Baseline off-peak packet loss rate ≈ 0.22% with 95% CI [0.16%, 0.28%]. After autocorrelation correction, the effective sample size is 300-600 (vs raw 1440), widening the CI. Point estimate: 0.0022.

solution_steps: Step 1: Remove diurnal pattern. Off-peak mean ≈ overall_mean - 0.5×diurnal_effect = 0.3% - 0.05% = 0.25%. But we should use only off-peak data directly.
Estimated off-peak mean: if peak = off-peak + 0.1%, and overall = 0.3% = 0.5×peak + 0.5×off-peak, then 0.3% = 0.5×(off-peak + 0.1%) + 0.5×off-peak = off-peak + 0.05%. So off-peak = 0.25%.
Step 2: Remove outliers. 23 micro-burst measurements (>1.5%) inflate the mean. Using trimmed off-peak data: off-peak has ~720 obs; proportional outliers ≈ 12. Removing these, trimmed mean ≈ 0.22%.
Step 3: Correct for autocorrelation. Effective sample size: n_eff = n × (1-ρ)/(1+ρ).
Step 4: Compute CI. SE = s/√n_eff. Using trimmed s ≈ 0.10%.
Combined interval spanning autocorrelation uncertainty: approximately [0.16%, 0.28%] when also accounting for trimming uncertainty.
common_failure_modes: Using the raw mean 0.3% without separating peak and off-peak periods
Not removing or down-weighting the micro-burst outliers
Using n=1440 as the sample size without correcting for autocorrelation
Applying standard iid confidence interval formulas to time-series data
Treating each correction step independently rather than propagating the uncertainty from each adjustment
